items.title: "There will never, never be a safe space in Gaza again – even after the war is over" | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The Israeli army is telling people to leave Gaza City as it expands its offensive in the region.

The military is calling on residents to move to an area described as a 'humanitarian zone' in the Mawasi area of Gaza. Israel has said it will target more high-rise buildings in the city which it says are being used by Hamas. One structure has been flattened, with a second also coming under attack. 

Gaza's health ministry said that 68 people have been killed in the past 24 hours. 

00:00 The irreparable damage that has been done to Gaza
00:34 Dan Stewart, Save the Children

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Island Hiiumaa off Estonia fears Russian attack as NATO conducts training exercices | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>NATO is conducting training exercises in the Baltic Sea for a reaction to a potential Russian attack. The Estonian island of Hiiumaa lies directly on the shipping route between the Russian city of St. Petersburg and the Russian enclave of Kaliningrad.

items.title: Roma women in Slovakia: The lasting trauma of being sterilized without consent | Focus on Europe
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Thousands of Roma women in Slovakia were sterilized without their consent. In Roma culture, large families are a sign of wealth and happiness. The government has since apologized, but the victims are demanding compensation.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Israeli army urges Gaza evacuation ahead of offensive | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Israel's army has urged the residents of Gaza City to leave the area, as it expands its military activities in the north of the enclave.

A spokesman for the military called on residents to move to a humanitarian zone being established in the Deir al-Balah area in the center of Gaza. This comes after Israel said on Friday it would start targeting high-rise buildings in the city it says are being used by Hamas. Gazan health officials said at least 27 people were killed throughout the city on Friday.

DW's Middle East Analyst Shani Rozanes has more. 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Why are so many countries in Africa either in or very close to being in debt distress? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>African countries are experiencing severe debt troubles with several governments having to sacrifice social spending to pay back creditors.

In Ghana, the government was spending more of its budget on health and education at the start of the 2010s. But after the government borrowed money on the markets the interest payment burden began to grow. That's the red line gradually rising, falling and then spiking.

DW talks to Nzioka Waita about Africa's debt dilemma. He is Managing Director for Africa at the Tony Blair Institute which published a paper this year titled 'A New debt Deal for Africa: Breaking the Vicious Cycle.'

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: What's driving young Nigerians into the arms of extremists? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>This year armed Islamist groups in Nigeria have reportedly ramped up their attacks fuelling insecurity and displacement in the region. Nigeria's National Human Rights Commission says insurgents and bandits have killed more people in the first half of 2025 than in all of 2024. 
 
DW’s Fanny Facsar sent this report from Borno State, where the armed Islamist group Boko Haram and an Islamic State splinter group are active. 

DW is joined by Dr. Fatima Akilu in Abuja. She led Nigeria’s first national countering violent extremism programme. She is now the Executive Director of the Neem Foundation, an organisation that runs field programmes on rehabilitation, reintegration, mental health, and community resilience in the northeast of Nigeria. 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Massive immigration raid at Hyundai plant, most are Korean | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US immigration officials have detained some 475 people at a Hyundai vehicle factory in the state of Georgia. 

Hundreds of federal agents took part in the raid at the facility which was built by the Korean company to manufacture electric vehicles. Authorities say most of the workers detained were South Korean nationals. It's the latest in a long line of workplace raids conducted as part of the Trump administration's mass deportation agenda. Speaking to the press shortly after the operation, the US president praised the Immigration and Customs Enforcement agency, known as ICE. 

Journalist Fabian Kretschmer in Seoul has more on the reaction to the raids in South Korea.

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: US unemployment at highest level since 2021 | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>New data from the US indicates the labor market has slowed down in the US. 
The country added just 22,000 jobs in August while economists had expected more than three times that number. 

00:00 August jobs report shows cracks in US labor market
00:40 Abigail Hall, Economics professor, University of Tampa

items.title: Why is it important to find more water? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Scientists drilling off the northeastern coast of the US say they've discovered what's likely a hidden ocean of fresh water under the seabed. It could eventually provide drinking water for millions in need. Tapping it however poses a host of challenges.

00:00 Scientists find pockets of fresh water under seabed
02:23 Brandon Dugan, Co-chief scientist, Expedition 501

items.title: The main points in the leaked plan for Gaza | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>The Washington Post has published a document outlining a post-war plan for Gaza that is reportedly circulating in the White House. It's not clear if the plan reflects Trump’s policy. 
The proposal is called the “Gaza Reconstitution, Economic Acceleration and Transformation Trust," or "GREAT Trust." Over 38 pages, the document lays out how Palestinians living there will be relocated to other countries or designated restricted zones in the strip for at least ten years, while the territory is reconstructed and transformed into a trade, tech and tourism hub. 

00:00 Post-war plan for Gaza reconstruction leaked
02:57 Adham Sahloul, Adjunct Senior Fellow, CNAS

#gaza #trump #israel 
For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: Why Trump wants to rebrand the Pentagon | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>US President Donald Trump is preparing to sign an executive order, giving the Department of Defense a secondary title, namely the Department of War. 
Permanently changing the name requires Congressional approval, but the president's order would authorize the use of the new label as a "secondary title" by his administration. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth will also reportedly be known as the Secretary of War.

00:00 Trump signs executive order to call Pentagon 'Department of War' 
02:59 Tyson Barker, Atlantic Council

#pentagon #trump #usa

items.title: What's really behind the UK's shoplifting surge? | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>It's been described as an "epidemic" and "out of control." So what's behind the perceived surge in shoplifting in the UK, Europe and the US - and what does it really mean for businesses? DW Business speaks with British criminologist Emmeline Taylor in London about the changing face of shoplifting and its effect on retailers.

items.title: Europe's fastest supercomputer 'Jupiter' launches in Germany | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>Europe's fastest supercomputer has officially booted up in western Germany as Europe looks to catch up to the US and China in the race for AI.

Chancellor Friedrich Merz said the Jupiter supercomputer will open new possibilities for training A-I models and scientific simulations. He hailed the machine as a symbol of Germany's leading role in the technological revolution, and said he hopes to turn the country into an 'AI nation'.

Here's a closer look at what is now the fourth-fastest supercomputer in the world.

00:00 Europe's fastes supercomputer launches in Germany
01:36 Steve Conway, Senior Analyst, Intersect360 Research

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/

items.title: EU hits Google with €2.95 billion fine over abusive online advertising practices | DW News
items.author: DW News
items.content:
<article>European Union regulators have fined Google almost 3 billion euros for breaching competition rules.

The US tech giant is accused of putting competitors at an unfair disadvantage by favoring its own digital ad business. Google now has sixty days to submit plans on how it will adjust its business model - though the European Union has retreated from threats to break up the company. Google says it will appeal the decision. 

For more news go to: http://www.dw.com/en/
